title: The Kondo effect in ferromagnetic quantum critical CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$
authors: Martin Sundermann, Joe D. Thompson, Eric D. Bauer, Chun Fu Chang, Sheng-Huai Chen, Chang-Yang Kuo, Liu Hao Tjeng, Getrud Zwicknagl, Andrea Severing
abstract: The mechanism of a pressure-induced quantum critical point in the heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$ has attracted interest, as ferromagnetic quantum criticality in a clean itinerant Ce compound is typically avoided. The localized versus itinerant character of the 4\textit{f} electrons is a key aspect for understanding this behavior. We investigated the electronic structure of the 4\textit{f} shell in CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$ using core-level photoelectron and x-ray absorption spectroscopy, demonstrating the hybridization of Ce 4\textit{f} with the conduction electrons. Linearly polarized x-ray absorption reveals a temperature-dependent linear dichroism consistent with the crystal-electric-field (CEF) sequence as inferred from the static susceptibility. This dichroism cannot be described by an ionic full-multiplet model alone, but is reproduced by including the Kondo effect within a single-impurity Anderson model in the non crossing approximation (SIAM/NCA). The Kondo effect mixes higher lying crystal-field states into a resulting multiorbital ground state with 4\textit{f} occupancy \textit{n}$_f$\,$\sim$\,0.9. Deviations at low temperatures between the measured linear dichroism and calculated dichroism suggest an orbital-dependent Kondo effect. A scenario in which there is a multiorbital ground state and orbital-dependent Kondo hybridization should be a starting point for a model of pressure-induced criticality in CeRh$_6$Ge$_4$.
summary: This paper investigates the electronic structure of the heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh6Ge4 near a quantum critical point. By analyzing X-ray absorption spectroscopy and photoelectron data, the authors demonstrate that simple models fail to explain temperature dependence. They successfully model the results by incorporating the Kondo effect into a multiorbital ground state description, highlighting orbital-dependent correlations as key to understanding its magnetic criticality.
main_problem: The study aims to understand the mechanism behind a pressure-induced quantum critical point (QCP) in the heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh6Ge4, focusing on the interplay between localized and itinerant 4f electron character.
main_result: The experimental data are best explained by incorporating the Kondo effect into a multiorbital ground state model, suggesting an orbital-dependent hybridization mechanism is crucial for describing the low-temperature magnetic behavior.
method: The research combines advanced spectroscopic measurements (PES and XAS) with theoretical modeling using full-multiplet configuration interaction (fm-CI) and simplified Anderson Impurity Models (SIAM/NCA).
model_or_system: The system is the heavy fermion ferromagnet CeRh6Ge4, which exhibits complex magnetic ordering influenced by strong electron correlations. The model treats the hybridization of Ce 4f states with conduction electrons within a multi-orbital framework.
key_observables: Temperature dependence of linear dichroism (LD), spectral weights in XAS/PES satellites, and the derived Kondo temperature (T_K).
important_parameters: The critical pressure ($p_c = 0.8 	ext{ GPa}$), the effective $4f$ occupancy ($n_f \sim 0.9$), and the Kondo coupling strength ($T_K \sim 60 	ext{ K}$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on approximations such as the Non-Crossing Approximation (NCA) for the SIAM, and assumes specific symmetries or constraints when fitting spectroscopic data.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the temperature dependence of experimental LD versus theoretical predictions derived from both simple thermal occupation models and more complex Kondo-affected multiorbital calculations.
paper_structure: The paper progresses by first establishing evidence for $4f$ hybridization via PES/XAS, then using linear dichroism measurements to constrain a CEF model, and finally employing the SIAM/NCA framework to incorporate the temperature-dependent Kondo screening effect into the ground state description.
why_interesting: This work provides a detailed spectroscopic pathway to map out the complex many-body physics governing quantum criticality in heavy fermion systems, directly linking measurable optical properties (dichroism) to fundamental correlated parameters like $T_K$.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ce 3d core level PES data of CeRh6Ge4 (black circles) after background correction, plus the result of a full-multiplet configuration-interaction (FM-CI) simulation (red line). The gray ruler at the bottom indicates the energy positions of the I(fn) spectral weights.

FIG. 2. Isotropic XAS spectra, Iiso = 1/3·(I(E ∥c) + 2·I(E ⊥c)), of CeRh6Ge4 at the Ce M 4,5 edges for several temperatures T between 20 and 200 K. Insets: enlarged view of 4f 0 satellites at the high energy tail of the M 5 and M 4 edges.

FIG. 3. (a) Linear polarized XAS spectra, I(E ∥c) (red) and I(E ⊥c) (blue), of CeRh6Ge4 at the Ce M 4,5 edges at T = 20K and the corresponding linear dichroism LDexp = I(E ∥c) - I(E ⊥c) (green). (b) Simulated linear po- larized XAS spectra of pure |±jz⟩states. (c) Simulated linear dichroism of pure |±jz⟩states, LDjz, compared to experimen- tal LDexp (green dotted curve taken from (a)).

FIG. 4. (a) Temperature dependence of the experimental linear dichroism, LDexp(T). (b) Simulated LD(T) based on thermal occupation of states using the CEF model as described in the text, corresponding to TK = 0 K and ntot f (T) = 1. (c) Simulated LD(T) taking into account the Kondo effect in addition to thermal occupation of states, see text. (d) Occupation of states as function of temperature for pure thermal occupation (dashed lines) and in the presence of the Kondo effect with TK = 60 K and ntot f (T=0) = 0.9 (solid lines). The black lines are the total occupation ntot f (T) of the 4f shell with (solid) and without (dashed) Kondo effect. In the T = 0 K limit, 35% of the 4f shell...
